summary: The notebook demonstrates a minimalist, robust baseline for the NeurIPS 2025 Open Polymer competition by training a single CatBoost regressor for each of the five polymer property targets. It emphasizes stability over complexity, using automated Mordred molecular descriptors and implementing a skew-aware median/mean imputation strategy for missing values. The author shows that careful handling of distributional skew and simple physical clipping of predictions can yield a competitive leaderboard score without ensembling or advanced weighting. Readers should take away the value of keeping pipelines simple, validating locally with holdouts, and respecting domain constraints during post-processing.
competition_flow: Loads raw CSV target files and test SMILES, computes Mordred descriptors, trains a unified CatBoost regressor per target with skew-aware imputation, applies physical/clipping post-processing, and exports a multi-target submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads target CSVs (desc_tg.csv, desc_tc.csv, desc_rg.csv, desc_ffv.csv, desc_de.csv) and test CSV with pd.read_csv(low_memory=False).
Converts test SMILES strings to RDKit Mol objects for descriptor calculation.
data_processing: Drops constant columns and filters for numeric types only.
Replaces infinities with NaNs across train and test sets.
Imputes missing values using median (for features with skew > 1.0) or mean (otherwise).
Converts arrays to float32 and clips predictions to training 1-99% percentiles and physical bounds (FFV: [0,1], Density: [0, inf)).
insights: Using a skew-aware imputation strategy (median for highly skewed features, mean otherwise) improves robustness over naive filling methods.
Clipping predictions to the training distribution's 1-99% percentile prevents extreme outliers without requiring complex post-processing.
A unified, minimal CatBoost configuration across all targets simplifies tuning and maintains training stability.
